OT4ML
Optimal Transport for Machine Learners
Chapters
Optimal Matching between Point CloudsMonge Problem between MeasuresKantorovich RelaxationDual ProblemSemi-discrete and Wasserstein-1Divergences and Dual NormsEntropic Regularization: Sinkhorn AlgorithmEntropic Regularization: ConvergenceGeneralized Wasserstein DistancesGeneralized OT ProblemsBeyond Comparing MeasuresDynamic Optimal TransportWasserstein Gradient FlowsGenerative Models via Transportation
ConclusionNotation Table

Beyond Comparing Measures

The last group leaves the setting of scalar measures on a common ambient space. Vector- and matrix-valued OT transports mass with internal degrees of freedom, Gromov--Wasserstein compares metric-measure spaces without a prescribed correspondence, and quantum OT replaces scalar couplings by positive operators. In each case, the transport plan also has to encode structure carried by the support, the fibers, or the non-commutative state space.

Guiding Comparison

Classical OT compares two scalar measures through a ground cost. Vector- and matrix-valued OT keeps the base space but enriches the mass attached to each point. Gromov--Wasserstein removes the common base space and compares internal distances. Quantum OT keeps the marginal idea but replaces vectors and matrices of masses by positive operators and partial traces.

from pathlib import Path
import sys

from IPython.display import Image as DisplayImage
from IPython.display import display

here = Path.cwd()
myst_dir = None
for candidate in [here, here.parent, here / "myst", here.parent / "myst", here.parent.parent / "myst"]:
    if (candidate / "ot4ml_web.py").exists():
        myst_dir = candidate.resolve()
        sys.path.insert(0, str(myst_dir))
        break

if myst_dir is None:
    raise RuntimeError("Could not locate myst/ot4ml_web.py")

repo_root = myst_dir.parent
thumbnails = repo_root / "notebooks-figures" / "thumbnails"

def show_book_figure(name, width=760):
    display(DisplayImage(filename=str(thumbnails / f"{name}.png"), width=width))

Vector and Matrix-Valued Measures¶

Scalar OT transports a nonnegative density. In imaging, color processing, spectral analysis, diffusion tensor imaging and quantum-inspired models, the object attached to a point can instead have several nonnegative components or a positive semidefinite matrix. The first step beyond scalar OT is the positive vector-valued case, where the fiber remains linear and commutative but the channels may interact.

Positive Vector-Valued Measures¶

Definition: Positive Vector-Valued Measure

A positive R+m\RR_+^mR+m​-valued measure on X\XX is a tuple

μ=(μ1,…,μm)∈M+(X;R+m),\mu=(\mu^1,\ldots,\mu^m)\in\mathcal M_+(\X;\RR_+^m),μ=(μ1,…,μm)∈M+​(X;R+m​),
(1)

where each component μk\mu^kμk is a nonnegative finite measure.

This models multi-channel densities such as color histograms, spectral bins or several species transported on the same domain. In a conservative model the mass of each channel is preserved, so one assumes μ0k(X)=μ1k(X)\mu_0^k(\X)=\mu_1^k(\X)μ0k​(X)=μ1k​(X) for every kkk. The natural vector-valued extension therefore starts from the positive cone R+m\RR_+^mR+m​.

To keep the notation readable, first assume that the endpoints and the curve have densities. The direct analogue of Benamou--Brenier fixes a vector density ut(x)∈R+mu_t(x)\in\RR_+^mut​(x)∈R+m​ and a spatial flux Vt(x)=(Vt,1,…,Vt,d)∈(Rm)dV_t(x)=(V_{t,1},\ldots,V_{t,d})\in(\RR^m)^dVt​(x)=(Vt,1​,…,Vt,d​)∈(Rm)d, where Vt,ℓkV_{t,\ell}^kVt,ℓk​ is the momentum of channel kkk in spatial direction ℓ\ellℓ. The conservative vector transport cost associated with an action density Φ\PhiΦ is

WΦ2(μ0,μ1):=inf⁡u,V∫01 ⁣∫XΦ(ut(x),Vt(x))  dx  dt\mathcal W_{\Phi}^2(\mu_0,\mu_1) \eqdef \inf_{u,V} \int_0^1\!\int_\X \Phi(u_t(x),V_t(x))\,\d x\,\d tWΦ2​(μ0​,μ1​):=u,Vinf​∫01​∫X​Φ(ut​(x),Vt​(x))dxdt
(2)

subject to the endpoint constraints u0 dx=μ0u_0\d x=\mu_0u0​dx=μ0​, u1 dx=μ1u_1\d x=\mu_1u1​dx=μ1​ and the componentwise continuity equation

∂tut+∇x⋅Vt=0,(∇x⋅Vt)k=∑ℓ=1d∂xℓVt,ℓk.\partial_t u_t+\nabla_x\cdot V_t=0, \qquad (\nabla_x\cdot V_t)^k = \sum_{\ell=1}^d\partial_{x_\ell}V_{t,\ell}^k.∂t​ut​+∇x​⋅Vt​=0,(∇x​⋅Vt​)k=ℓ=1∑d​∂xℓ​​Vt,ℓk​.
(3)

Thus each component satisfies its own continuity equation, but the cost may still couple the components. Singular curves are handled as in scalar dynamic OT by replacing densities and fluxes by measures and using the lower semicontinuous perspective recession convention.

A simple quadratic family is obtained from a mobility matrix M(u)∈S+m\mathsf M(u)\in\mathbb S_+^mM(u)∈S+m​:

ΦM(u,V)=∑ℓ=1dVℓ⊤M(u)†Vℓ,\Phi_{\mathsf M}(u,V) = \sum_{\ell=1}^d V_\ell^\top \mathsf M(u)^\dagger V_\ell,ΦM​(u,V)=ℓ=1∑d​Vℓ⊤​M(u)†Vℓ​,
(4)

with the usual convention that the value is finite only when each VℓV_\ellVℓ​ belongs to the range of M(u)\mathsf M(u)M(u). One chooses M\mathsf MM so that this matrix perspective is convex and one-homogeneous in (u,V)(u,V)(u,V); this holds for the linear positive mobilities below. For m=1m=1m=1 and M(u)=u\mathsf M(u)=uM(u)=u, one recovers exactly the scalar Benamou--Brenier action. For

Mdiag(u)=diag⁡(u1,…,um),\mathsf M_{\mathrm{diag}}(u)=\operatorname{diag}(u_1,\ldots,u_m),Mdiag​(u)=diag(u1​,…,um​),
(5)

the channels move independently. Non-diagonal mobilities are the simplest way to couple the coordinates while keeping the same componentwise conservation law. For instance, with q=m−1/2(1,…,1)q=m^{-1/2}(1,\ldots,1)q=m−1/2(1,…,1) and κ≥0\kappa\geq0κ≥0,

Mκ(u)=diag⁡(u)+κ(∑k=1muk)qq⊤\mathsf M_\kappa(u) = \operatorname{diag}(u) + \kappa\left(\sum_{k=1}^m u_k\right)qq^\topMκ​(u)=diag(u)+κ(k=1∑m​uk​)qq⊤
(6)

increases the mobility in the common channel direction qqq while leaving transverse directions controlled by the diagonal part. The local cost of moving one component can therefore depend on the densities and momenta of the other components, even though each component mass remains conserved.

Proposition: Diagonal Positive Vector Benamou--Brenier

Assume that μ0k,μ1k∈M+(X)\mu_0^k,\mu_1^k\in\mathcal M_+(\X)μ0k​,μ1k​∈M+​(X) have the same mass mkm_kmk​ for every kkk. For the diagonal mobility Mdiag\mathsf M_{\mathrm{diag}}Mdiag​,

Wdiag2(μ0,μ1)=∑k:mk>0mk W22 ⁣(μ0kmk,μ1kmk),\mathcal W_{\mathrm{diag}}^2(\mu_0,\mu_1) = \sum_{k:m_k>0} m_k\, \Wass_2^2\!\left( \frac{\mu_0^k}{m_k}, \frac{\mu_1^k}{m_k} \right),Wdiag2​(μ0​,μ1​)=k:mk​>0∑​mk​W22​(mk​μ0k​​,mk​μ1k​​),
(7)

with the convention that zero-mass channels contribute zero.

Proof

For Mdiag(u)=diag⁡(u1,…,um)\mathsf M_{\mathrm{diag}}(u)=\operatorname{diag}(u_1,\ldots,u_m)Mdiag​(u)=diag(u1​,…,um​), the action separates as

∑ℓ=1dVℓ⊤Mdiag(u)†Vℓ=∑k=1m∣Vk∣2uk,\sum_{\ell=1}^d V_\ell^\top\mathsf M_{\mathrm{diag}}(u)^\dagger V_\ell = \sum_{k=1}^m\frac{|V^k|^2}{u^k},ℓ=1∑d​Vℓ⊤​Mdiag​(u)†Vℓ​=k=1∑m​uk∣Vk∣2​,
(8)

where Vk=(V1k,…,Vdk)V^k=(V_1^k,\ldots,V_d^k)Vk=(V1k​,…,Vdk​) is the spatial momentum of channel kkk. The constraint (3) also separates into ∂tuk+∇⋅Vk=0\partial_t u^k+\nabla\cdot V^k=0∂t​uk+∇⋅Vk=0. The minimization therefore splits into mmm independent scalar Benamou--Brenier problems. If mk>0m_k>0mk​>0, normalizing ρtk=utk/mk\rho_t^k=u_t^k/m_kρtk​=utk​/mk​ and ptk=Vtk/mkp_t^k=V_t^k/m_kptk​=Vtk​/mk​ factors the channel action as mk∫∣ptk∣2/ρtkm_k\int |p_t^k|^2/\rho_t^kmk​∫∣ptk​∣2/ρtk​, hence the scalar value is the displayed mkW22m_k\Wass_2^2mk​W22​ term. If mk=0m_k=0mk​=0, nonnegativity and conservation force the whole channel to vanish. Summing over all channels proves the claim.

The conservative positive-cone model above is the basic extension of Benamou--Brenier. Adding a source term ∂tu+∇⋅V=S\partial_t u+\nabla\cdot V=S∂t​u+∇⋅V=S and a convex perspective penalty in SSS gives unbalanced or reaction--transport variants Maas et al., 2015Maas et al., 2016Dolbeault et al., 2009Mielke, 2013. These generalized transport models include dissipation and density modulation. The figure below contrasts the exact diagonal case κ=0\kappa=0κ=0, where each positive channel is transported by its quantile map, with a large-κ\kappaκ illustrative common-mode interpolation in which the channels move more coherently. The endpoints are two-mode mixtures: at each spatial mode the two channels have Gaussian profiles with the same center but different amplitudes.

show_book_figure("vector-valued-measure-geodesics")

One-dimensional positive R+2\RR_+^2R+2​-valued transport displayed by arrow glyphs at eight time levels. Each endpoint is a mixture of two localized Gaussian modes, and, inside each mode, both channel profiles have the same center. Each arrow is proportional to the local fiber value (ut1(x),ut2(x))(u_t^1(x),u_t^2(x))(ut1​(x),ut2​(x)), and time runs vertically from the red source to the blue target. Left: for κ=0\kappa=0κ=0, the diagonal mobility gives two independent scalar quantile geodesics. Right: a large-κ\kappaκ common-mode interpolation bends the display toward q=2−1/2(1,1)q=2^{-1/2}(1,1)q=2−1/2(1,1), illustrating the effect of a mobility that favors coherent channel motion while keeping the same componentwise continuity equation.

The interactive demo keeps the same glyph idea and lets the coupling strength bend the fibers toward a common channel direction.

Interactive panel. Use the coupling and mixture controls to see how vector-valued mass transports both location and channel composition.

Positive Matrix-Valued Measures¶

The next simplest fiber is the positive matrix cone. This is the simplest tensor-valued model beyond vectors: the diagonal entries behave like positive channels, while the eigenvectors encode local orientations.

Definition: Positive Matrix-Valued Measure

Write Sm\mathbb S^mSm for real symmetric matrices and S+m\mathbb S_+^mS+m​ for the positive semidefinite cone. A positive S+m\mathbb S_+^mS+m​-valued measure is an element

A∈M+(X;S+m).\mathcal A\in\mathcal M_+(\X;\mathbb S_+^m).A∈M+​(X;S+m​).
(9)

If A\mathcal AA has density A(x)⪰0A(x)\succeq0A(x)⪰0, then tr⁡A(x)\operatorname{tr}A(x)trA(x) is the scalar amount of mass at xxx, while, wherever tr⁡A(x)>0\operatorname{tr}A(x)>0trA(x)>0, the normalized matrix A(x)/tr⁡A(x)A(x)/\operatorname{tr}A(x)A(x)/trA(x) records an internal covariance or orientation. This is the matrix analogue of the positive vector case: diagonal matrices encode nonnegative vector components, and non-diagonal matrices add a local eigenbasis.

The conservative Benamou--Brenier model fixes a matrix density At(x)∈S+mA_t(x)\in\mathbb S_+^mAt​(x)∈S+m​ and symmetric matrix fluxes Pt(x)=(Pt,1,…,Pt,d)∈(Sm)dP_t(x)=(P_{t,1},\ldots,P_{t,d})\in(\mathbb S^m)^dPt​(x)=(Pt,1​,…,Pt,d​)∈(Sm)d. With no flux through the boundary of X\XX, the full matrix mass ∫XAt(x) dx\int_\X A_t(x)\d x∫X​At​(x)dx is conserved, so the endpoints must have the same total matrix. The model minimizes the matrix-perspective action

Wmat2(A0,A1):=inf⁡A,P∫01 ⁣∫X∑ℓ=1dtr⁡ ⁣(Pt,ℓ⊤At†Pt,ℓ) dx  dt\mathcal W_{\mathrm{mat}}^2(\mathcal A_0,\mathcal A_1) \eqdef \inf_{A,P} \int_0^1\!\int_\X \sum_{\ell=1}^d \operatorname{tr}\!\left(P_{t,\ell}^{\top} A_t^\dagger P_{t,\ell}\right) \d x\,\d tWmat2​(A0​,A1​):=A,Pinf​∫01​∫X​ℓ=1∑d​tr(Pt,ℓ⊤​At†​Pt,ℓ​)dxdt
(10)

subject to A0 dx=A0A_0\d x=\mathcal A_0A0​dx=A0​, A1 dx=A1A_1\d x=\mathcal A_1A1​dx=A1​ and to the matrix-valued continuity equation

∂tAt+∇x⋅Pt=0,∇x⋅Pt=∑ℓ=1d∂xℓPt,ℓ.\partial_t A_t+\nabla_x\cdot P_t=0, \qquad \nabla_x\cdot P_t = \sum_{\ell=1}^d\partial_{x_\ell}P_{t,\ell}.∂t​At​+∇x​⋅Pt​=0,∇x​⋅Pt​=ℓ=1∑d​∂xℓ​​Pt,ℓ​.
(11)

Here A†A^\daggerA† denotes the Moore--Penrose inverse, with the usual perspective convention: the action is finite only when the columns of each Pt,ℓP_{t,\ell}Pt,ℓ​ belong to the range of AtA_tAt​. The map (A,P)↦tr⁡(P⊤A†P)(A,P)\mapsto\operatorname{tr}(P^\top A^\dagger P)(A,P)↦tr(P⊤A†P) is the matrix fractional function and is jointly convex on A⪰0A\succeq0A⪰0. This gives the simplest non-trivial matrix-valued transport model: spatial motion is conservative, but the fiber carries orientation through the eigenvectors of At(x)A_t(x)At​(x).

Proposition: Diagonal Matrix Subproblem

Assume that the endpoints are diagonal in a fixed orthonormal basis,

Ai=diag⁡(μi1,…,μim),i=0,1,\mathcal A_i=\operatorname{diag}(\mu_i^1,\ldots,\mu_i^m), \qquad i=0,1,Ai​=diag(μi1​,…,μim​),i=0,1,
(12)

and that μ0k(X)=μ1k(X)=mk\mu_0^k(\X)=\mu_1^k(\X)=m_kμ0k​(X)=μ1k​(X)=mk​ for every kkk. If one restricts the admissible curves in (10) to remain diagonal in that basis,

At=diag⁡(ut1,…,utm),Pt,ℓ=diag⁡(Vt,ℓ1,…,Vt,ℓm),A_t=\operatorname{diag}(u_t^1,\ldots,u_t^m), \qquad P_{t,\ell}=\operatorname{diag}(V_{t,\ell}^1,\ldots,V_{t,\ell}^m),At​=diag(ut1​,…,utm​),Pt,ℓ​=diag(Vt,ℓ1​,…,Vt,ℓm​),
(13)

then the restricted matrix problem has value

∑k:mk>0mk W22 ⁣(μ0kmk,μ1kmk).\sum_{k:m_k>0} m_k\, \Wass_2^2\!\left( \frac{\mu_0^k}{m_k}, \frac{\mu_1^k}{m_k} \right).k:mk​>0∑​mk​W22​(mk​μ0k​​,mk​μ1k​​).
(14)

with zero contribution from zero-mass channels. Thus the commuting matrix submodel is exactly the diagonal positive vector-valued Benamou--Brenier model.

Proof

The continuity equation (11) is diagonal entry by diagonal entry and gives ∂tuk+∇⋅Vk=0\partial_t u^k+\nabla\cdot V^k=0∂t​uk+∇⋅Vk=0. Moreover,

∑ℓ=1dtr⁡ ⁣(Pt,ℓ⊤At†Pt,ℓ)=∑k=1m∣Vtk∣2utk,\sum_{\ell=1}^d \operatorname{tr}\!\left(P_{t,\ell}^{\top}A_t^\dagger P_{t,\ell}\right) = \sum_{k=1}^m\frac{|V_t^k|^2}{u_t^k},ℓ=1∑d​tr(Pt,ℓ⊤​At†​Pt,ℓ​)=k=1∑m​utk​∣Vtk​∣2​,
(15)

with the same scalar perspective convention as before. The admissible set and the action are therefore exactly those of the diagonal vector model.

The restriction to a fixed diagonal basis gives eigenvalue transport; it should be read as a commuting submodel, not as a claim that non-diagonal excursions can never change the unrestricted value. The genuinely matrix-valued case starts when the eigenspaces vary with xxx or along the interpolation, so that the transported object carries both mass and orientation. Static matrix-valued Monge--Kantorovich problems and dual test-function metrics were developed in Ning & Georgiou, 2014Jiang et al., 2012Ning et al., 2015; dynamic versions and related non-commutative geometries appear in Chen et al., 2016Chen et al., 2020Carlen & Maas, 2014Peyré et al., 2019. The figure below shows the analogous independent/coupled contrast for positive 2×22\times22×2 matrix fibers, using two localized matrix modes whose eigenvalue profiles share a common center at each mode.

show_book_figure("matrix-valued-measure-geodesic")

Positive 2×22\times22×2 matrix-valued transport on a one-dimensional base. Each endpoint is a mixture of two localized matrix modes; within one mode, both eigenvalue profiles are Gaussian bumps with the same center. Each ellipse is the glyph of a positive semidefinite matrix At(x)A_t(x)At​(x), with axes given by eigenvectors and eigenvalues. Left: the matrices are diagonal in a fixed basis, giving the commuting tensor analogue of independent vector channels. Right: a coupled illustrative interpolation bends packet motion toward the trace-density transport and uses non-commuting eigendirections; the superposition remains positive semidefinite and produces spatially varying orientations.

Interactive panel. Use the coupling and rotation controls to compare matrix-valued transport of anisotropic local structure.

Gromov--Wasserstein¶

Gromov--Wasserstein compares spaces through their internal distance structures rather than through a fixed ambient ground cost. This is the right extension for graphs, shapes and point clouds whose points are not pre-aligned.

Discrete Formulation¶

Optimal transport needs a ground cost CCC to compare histograms (a,b)(a,b)(a,b), and thus cannot be used directly if the histograms are not defined on the same underlying space, or if one cannot pre-register these spaces to define a ground cost. Instead, assume that two matrices D∈Rn×nD\in\RR^{n\times n}D∈Rn×n and D′∈Rm×mD'\in\RR^{m\times m}D′∈Rm×m represent relationships between points. A typical scenario is when these matrices are powers of distance matrices. The discrete Gromov--Wasserstein problem reads

GW⁡((a,D),(b,D′))p:=min⁡P∈U(a,b)∑i,j,i′,j′Δ(Di,i′,Dj,j′′)pPi,jPi′,j′,\operatorname{GW}((a,D),(b,D'))^p \eqdef \min_{P\in\mathbf U(a,b)} \sum_{i,j,i',j'} \Delta(D_{i,i'},D'_{j,j'})^p P_{i,j}P_{i',j'},GW((a,D),(b,D′))p:=P∈U(a,b)min​i,j,i′,j′∑​Δ(Di,i′​,Dj,j′′​)pPi,j​Pi′,j′​,
(16)

where p≥1p\geq1p≥1 and Δ\DeltaΔ is usually Δ(u,v)=∣u−v∣\Delta(u,v)=|u-v|Δ(u,v)=∣u−v∣. This is a non-convex quadratic problem over the transport polytope. In the uniform case with m=nm=nm=n and PPP constrained to be a permutation matrix, it becomes a Quadratic Assignment Problem, already NP-hard in full generality Loiola et al., 2007. The relaxed coupling formulation can therefore be read as a soft graph-matching model Lyzinski et al., 2016.

show_book_figure("gromov-isometry-matching")

Gromov--Wasserstein correspondences under increasing deformation. The red and blue point clouds are not compared through an ambient Euclidean cross-cost; instead, the GW coupling compares their internal pairwise distances. A perfectly isometric copy admits a clean structural match, while mild and deliberately stronger deformations progressively bend the correspondence.

The interactive demo uses a fixed structural correspondence and lets the deformation change the pairwise-distance residual. This isolates the quantity minimized by the GW objective.

Interactive panel. Use the deformation and point controls to inspect correspondences when only within-space distances are meaningful.

When D,D′D,D'D,D′ are genuine distance matrices, the construction below defines a distance between metric spaces equipped with a probability distribution, up to measure-preserving isometries Mémoli, 2011Sturm, 2012Schmitzer & Schnörr, 2013. The same construction also explains why GW satisfies the triangle inequality after quotienting by isometries, and its relation to Hausdorff and Gromov--Hausdorff distances is discussed at the end of the section.

General Setting¶

Definition: Metric-Measure Space

A metric-measure space is a triple

X=(X,dX,α),\mathbb X=(\X,d_\X,\alpha),X=(X,dX​,α),
(17)

where (X,dX)(\X,d_\X)(X,dX​) is a metric space and α\alphaα is a probability measure on X\XX.

The natural setting is that of Polish metric spaces; compactness is often assumed when one wants existence and a clean metric statement without adding tightness hypotheses.

For metric-measure spaces X=(X,dX,α)\mathbb X=(\X,d_\X,\alpha)X=(X,dX​,α) and Y=(Y,dY,β)\mathbb Y=(\Y,d_\Y,\beta)Y=(Y,dY​,β), define

GW⁡(X,Y)p:=min⁡π∈Π(α,β)∫X2×Y2Δ(dX(x,x′),dY(y,y′))p dπ(x,y) dπ(x′,y′).\operatorname{GW}(\mathbb X,\mathbb Y)^p \eqdef \min_{\pi\in\Couplings(\alpha,\beta)} \int_{\X^2\times\Y^2} \Delta(d_\X(x,x'),d_\Y(y,y'))^p \d\pi(x,y)\d\pi(x',y').GW(X,Y)p:=π∈Π(α,β)min​∫X2×Y2​Δ(dX​(x,x′),dY​(y,y′))pdπ(x,y)dπ(x′,y′).
(18)
Proposition: Euclidean GW Is Controlled by Wasserstein

Let α,β\alpha,\betaα,β be probability measures on Rd\RR^dRd, equipped with the Euclidean distance, and take Δ(u,v)=∣u−v∣\Delta(u,v)=|u-v|Δ(u,v)=∣u−v∣ in (18). Then

GW⁡((Rd,∥⋅∥,α),(Rd,∥⋅∥,β))≤2Wp(α,β).\operatorname{GW}((\RR^d,\norm{\cdot},\alpha),(\RR^d,\norm{\cdot},\beta)) \leq 2\Wass_p(\alpha,\beta).GW((Rd,∥⋅∥,α),(Rd,∥⋅∥,β))≤2Wp​(α,β).
(19)
Proof

Let π\piπ be any coupling between α\alphaα and β\betaβ. For two independent pairs (X,Y),(X′,Y′)∼π(X,Y),(X',Y')\sim\pi(X,Y),(X′,Y′)∼π, the reverse triangle inequality gives

∣∥X−X′∥−∥Y−Y′∥∣≤∥X−Y∥+∥X′−Y′∥.\left|\norm{X-X'}-\norm{Y-Y'}\right| \leq \norm{X-Y}+\norm{X'-Y'}.∣∥X−X′∥−∥Y−Y′∥∣≤∥X−Y∥+∥X′−Y′∥.
(20)

Taking the LpL^pLp norm and using Minkowski gives a bound by 2(∫∥x−y∥p dπ)1/p2(\int\norm{x-y}^p\d\pi)^{1/p}2(∫∥x−y∥pdπ)1/p. Optimizing over π\piπ proves the claim.

Definition: Isometric Metric-Measure Spaces

Two metric-measure spaces X=(X,dX,α)\mathbb X=(\X,d_\X,\alpha)X=(X,dX​,α) and Y=(Y,dY,β)\mathbb Y=(\Y,d_\Y,\beta)Y=(Y,dY​,β) are isometric if there exists a measurable map ϕ:supp⁡(α)→supp⁡(β)\phi:\operatorname{supp}(\alpha)\to\operatorname{supp}(\beta)ϕ:supp(α)→supp(β) such that ϕ♯α=β\phi_\sharp\alpha=\betaϕ♯​α=β, ϕ(supp⁡(α))=supp⁡(β)\phi(\operatorname{supp}(\alpha))=\operatorname{supp}(\beta)ϕ(supp(α))=supp(β), and

dY(ϕ(x),ϕ(x′))=dX(x,x′)d_\Y(\phi(x),\phi(x'))=d_\X(x,x')dY​(ϕ(x),ϕ(x′))=dX​(x,x′)
(21)

for all x,x′∈supp⁡(α)x,x'\in\operatorname{supp}(\alpha)x,x′∈supp(α).

Theorem: Gromov--Wasserstein Metric Modulo Isometries

For compact metric-measure spaces, p≥1p\geq1p≥1 and Δ(u,v)=∣u−v∣\Delta(u,v)=|u-v|Δ(u,v)=∣u−v∣, GW⁡\operatorname{GW}GW defines a distance up to measure-preserving isometries.

Proof

If GW⁡(X,Y)=0\operatorname{GW}(\mathbb X,\mathbb Y)=0GW(X,Y)=0 and π\piπ is optimal, then dX(x,x′)=dY(y,y′)d_\X(x,x')=d_\Y(y,y')dX​(x,x′)=dY​(y,y′) holds π⊗π\pi\otimes\piπ⊗π-almost everywhere. By continuity, this equality holds on supp⁡(π)2\operatorname{supp}(\pi)^2supp(π)2. Both X\mathbb XX and Y\mathbb YY are isometric to the support space (supp⁡(π),dπ,π)(\operatorname{supp}(\pi),d_\pi,\pi)(supp(π),dπ​,π), where

dπ((x,y),(x′,y′)):=12dX(x,x′)+12dY(y,y′).d_\pi((x,y),(x',y')) \eqdef \frac12d_\X(x,x')+\frac12d_\Y(y,y').dπ​((x,y),(x′,y′)):=21​dX​(x,x′)+21​dY​(y,y′).
(22)

The first projection is measure-preserving and distance-preserving on supp⁡(π)\operatorname{supp}(\pi)supp(π), and compactness gives surjectivity onto supp⁡(α)\operatorname{supp}(\alpha)supp(α); the same argument applies to the second projection.

For the triangle inequality, glue optimal couplings between X,Y\mathbb X,\mathbb YX,Y and between Y,Z\mathbb Y,\mathbb ZY,Z. The projected X×Z\X\times\ZX×Z marginal is feasible, and the pointwise triangle inequality together with Minkowski gives

GW⁡(X,Z)≤GW⁡(X,Y)+GW⁡(Y,Z).\operatorname{GW}(\mathbb X,\mathbb Z) \leq \operatorname{GW}(\mathbb X,\mathbb Y) + \operatorname{GW}(\mathbb Y,\mathbb Z).GW(X,Z)≤GW(X,Y)+GW(Y,Z).
(23)

Symmetry and non-negativity are immediate.

The metric structure also gives geodesics. Sturm’s construction allows one to speak about interpolation, barycenters and gradient flows directly on the space of metric-measure spaces, even though the intermediate space lives on a product support and is therefore expensive numerically Sturm, 2012.

Proposition: Gromov--Wasserstein Geodesics

Let X0=(X0,dX0,α0)\mathbb X_0=(\X_0,d_{\X_0},\alpha_0)X0​=(X0​,dX0​​,α0​) and X1=(X1,dX1,α1)\mathbb X_1=(\X_1,d_{\X_1},\alpha_1)X1​=(X1​,dX1​​,α1​) be compact metric-measure spaces, and let π⋆\pi^\starπ⋆ be an optimal coupling. Define, on Z=X0×X1\mathcal Z=\X_0\times\X_1Z=X0​×X1​,

dt((x0,x1),(x0′,x1′)):=(1−t)dX0(x0,x0′)+tdX1(x1,x1′),Xt=(Z,dt,π⋆).d_t((x_0,x_1),(x'_0,x'_1)) \eqdef (1-t)d_{\X_0}(x_0,x'_0) + t d_{\X_1}(x_1,x'_1), \qquad \mathbb X_t=(\mathcal Z,d_t,\pi^\star).dt​((x0​,x1​),(x0′​,x1′​)):=(1−t)dX0​​(x0​,x0′​)+tdX1​​(x1​,x1′​),Xt​=(Z,dt​,π⋆).
(24)

After quotienting zero-distance points when needed, t↦Xtt\mapsto\mathbb X_tt↦Xt​ is a constant-speed geodesic:

GW⁡(Xs,Xt)=∣t−s∣GW⁡(X0,X1).\operatorname{GW}(\mathbb X_s,\mathbb X_t) = |t-s|\operatorname{GW}(\mathbb X_0,\mathbb X_1).GW(Xs​,Xt​)=∣t−s∣GW(X0​,X1​).
(25)
Proof

For s<ts<ts<t, couple Xs\mathbb X_sXs​ and Xt\mathbb X_tXt​ by the diagonal coupling on Z\mathcal ZZ. The distance difference is exactly

dt(z,z′)−ds(z,z′)=(t−s)(dX1(x1,x1′)−dX0(x0,x0′)),d_t(z,z')-d_s(z,z') = (t-s)\big(d_{\X_1}(x_1,x'_1)-d_{\X_0}(x_0,x'_0)\big),dt​(z,z′)−ds​(z,z′)=(t−s)(dX1​​(x1​,x1′​)−dX0​​(x0​,x0′​)),
(26)

so GW⁡(Xs,Xt)≤(t−s)D\operatorname{GW}(\mathbb X_s,\mathbb X_t)\leq(t-s)DGW(Xs​,Xt​)≤(t−s)D, where D=GW⁡(X0,X1)D=\operatorname{GW}(\mathbb X_0,\mathbb X_1)D=GW(X0​,X1​). Applying the triangle inequality to X0,Xs,Xt,X1\mathbb X_0,\mathbb X_s,\mathbb X_t,\mathbb X_1X0​,Xs​,Xt​,X1​ gives the reverse bound.

show_book_figure("gromov-nonisometric-distortion")

Local distortion in a mildly non-isometric GW match. The left panel colors transport segments by the average residual induced by the displayed hard correspondence. The right panel shows the pairwise-distance residual matrix ∣dX(xi,xi′)−dY(yσ(i),yσ(i′))∣|d_\X(x_i,x_{i'})-d_\Y(y_{\sigma(i)},y_{\sigma(i')})|∣dX​(xi​,xi′​)−dY​(yσ(i)​,yσ(i′)​)∣, with darker entries marking larger local distortion. This matrix is the local contribution minimized by the discrete GW objective for the displayed correspondence.

Interactive panel. Use the deformation and shift controls to see where a Gromov-Wasserstein correspondence preserves or distorts pairwise distances.

Proposition: Memoli Profile Lower Bound

Let X=(X,dX,α)\mathbb X=(\X,d_\X,\alpha)X=(X,dX​,α) and Y=(Y,dY,β)\mathbb Y=(\Y,d_\Y,\beta)Y=(Y,dY​,β) be compact metric-measure spaces. For each x∈Xx\in\Xx∈X and y∈Yy\in\Yy∈Y, define the distance-profile measures on R+\RR_+R+​ by

αx:=(dX(x,⋅))♯α,βy:=(dY(y,⋅))♯β.\alpha_x\eqdef(d_\X(x,\cdot))_\sharp\alpha, \qquad \beta_y\eqdef(d_\Y(y,\cdot))_\sharp\beta.αx​:=(dX​(x,⋅))♯​α,βy​:=(dY​(y,⋅))♯​β.
(27)

Let EX=(x↦αx)♯α\mathsf E_\mathbb X=(x\mapsto\alpha_x)_\sharp\alphaEX​=(x↦αx​)♯​α and EY=(y↦βy)♯β\mathsf E_\mathbb Y=(y\mapsto\beta_y)_\sharp\betaEY​=(y↦βy​)♯​β. Then

Wp(EX,EY)≤GW⁡(X,Y),\Wass_p(\mathsf E_\mathbb X,\mathsf E_\mathbb Y) \leq \operatorname{GW}(\mathbb X,\mathbb Y),Wp​(EX​,EY​)≤GW(X,Y),
(28)

where the left-hand distance uses the one-dimensional Wasserstein distance between profile measures as its ground cost.

Proof

Fix any π∈Π(α,β)\pi\in\Couplings(\alpha,\beta)π∈Π(α,β). It induces a coupling (x,y)↦(αx,βy)(x,y)\mapsto(\alpha_x,\beta_y)(x,y)↦(αx​,βy​) between the profile laws, hence

Wp(EX,EY)p≤∫X×YWp(αx,βy)p  dπ(x,y).\Wass_p(\mathsf E_\mathbb X,\mathsf E_\mathbb Y)^p \leq \int_{\X\times\Y} \Wass_p(\alpha_x,\beta_y)^p\,\d\pi(x,y).Wp​(EX​,EY​)p≤∫X×Y​Wp​(αx​,βy​)pdπ(x,y).
(29)

For fixed (x,y)(x,y)(x,y), the map (x′,y′)↦(dX(x,x′),dY(y,y′))(x',y')\mapsto(d_\X(x,x'),d_\Y(y,y'))(x′,y′)↦(dX​(x,x′),dY​(y,y′)) pushes the same coupling π\piπ to a coupling between αx\alpha_xαx​ and βy\beta_yβy​. Integrating the resulting one-dimensional OT bound over (x,y)(x,y)(x,y) gives the GW objective for π\piπ. Taking the infimum over π\piπ proves the claim.

This lower bound is useful computationally because the profile cost matrix Cij=Wp(αxi,βyj)pC_{ij}=\Wass_p(\alpha_{x_i},\beta_{y_j})^pCij​=Wp​(αxi​​,βyj​​)p is an ordinary OT cost between points. Solving this easier OT problem gives a geometry-aware initialization for the non-convex GW iterations; it is the same idea used above as a useful initialization principle for a non-convex solver.

Entropic Regularization and Fused GW¶

For the common squared distortion Δ(u,v)2=(u−v)2\Delta(u,v)^2=(u-v)^2Δ(u,v)2=(u−v)2, one often computes a stationary point of the entropic relaxation

min⁡P∈U(a,b)ED,D′(P)−ϵH(P).\min_{P\in\mathbf U(a,b)} \mathcal E_{D,D'}(P)-\epsilon H(P).P∈U(a,b)min​ED,D′​(P)−ϵH(P).
(30)

Although the objective is non-convex, successive linearizations lead to a practical mirror-descent scheme Peyré et al., 2016. Up to an irrelevant global factor in the gradient, one alternates

P(ℓ+1):=min⁡P∈U(a,b)⟨P,C(ℓ)⟩−ϵH(P),P^{(\ell+1)} \eqdef \min_{P\in\mathbf U(a,b)} \langle P,C^{(\ell)}\rangle-\epsilon H(P),P(ℓ+1):=P∈U(a,b)min​⟨P,C(ℓ)⟩−ϵH(P),
(31)

with

C(ℓ):=D⊙2a 1m⊤+1n(D′⊙2b)⊤−2D P(ℓ) D′⊤.C^{(\ell)} \eqdef D^{\odot2}a\,\mathbf 1_m^\top + \mathbf 1_n(D'^{\odot2}b)^\top - 2D\,P^{(\ell)}\,D'^\top.C(ℓ):=D⊙2a1m⊤​+1n​(D′⊙2b)⊤−2DP(ℓ)D′⊤.
(32)

Each update is an ordinary entropic OT problem and can therefore be solved with Sinkhorn iterations. This improves scalability and smooths the landscape, but it does not remove the non-convexity of the GW objective. This is the standard entropic GW solver used to compute soft maps between domains.

Fused Gromov--Wasserstein augments the structural term with a feature transport cost Vayer et al., 2019. In the discrete case, given a cross-feature cost M∈Rn×mM\in\RR^{n\times m}M∈Rn×m and a parameter λ∈[0,1]\lambda\in[0,1]λ∈[0,1], one minimizes

FGW⁡λ,p((a,D),(b,D′))p:=min⁡P∈U(a,b)(1−λ)∑i,jMijPij+λ∑i,j,i′,j′Δ(Dii′,Djj′′)pPijPi′j′.\operatorname{FGW}_{\lambda,p}((a,D),(b,D'))^p \eqdef \min_{P\in\mathbf U(a,b)} (1-\lambda)\sum_{i,j}M_{ij}P_{ij} + \lambda \sum_{i,j,i',j'} \Delta(D_{ii'},D'_{jj'})^pP_{ij}P_{i'j'}.FGWλ,p​((a,D),(b,D′))p:=P∈U(a,b)min​(1−λ)i,j∑​Mij​Pij​+λi,j,i′,j′∑​Δ(Dii′​,Djj′′​)pPij​Pi′j′​.
(33)

The endpoints λ=0\lambda=0λ=0 and λ=1\lambda=1λ=1 recover feature-only OT and pure GW respectively; intermediate values trade attribute matching against structural matching. The first term compares node attributes in the usual OT sense, and the second compares intrinsic geometry; this is useful when two spaces have both distances and features, and the two sources of information may disagree.

show_book_figure("fused-gromov-feature-geometry")

Feature information and intrinsic geometry in fused Gromov--Wasserstein. Small inner disks encode binary node features. Feature-only OT follows the attributes even when this crosses the shape structure, pure GW follows the intrinsic ordering, and fused GW balances the feature term with the pairwise-distance distortion.

Interactive panel. Use the geometry-weight and feature-conflict controls to balance structural matching against feature agreement.

Hausdorff and Gromov--Hausdorff Viewpoints¶

If A,BA,BA,B are compact subsets of a common metric space (Z,dZ)(\mathcal Z,d_\mathcal Z)(Z,dZ​), their Hausdorff distance is

dHZ(A,B)=max⁡{sup⁡a∈Ainf⁡b∈BdZ(a,b),sup⁡b∈Binf⁡a∈AdZ(a,b)}.d_{\mathrm H}^{\mathcal Z}(A,B) = \max\left\{ \sup_{a\in A}\inf_{b\in B}d_\mathcal Z(a,b), \sup_{b\in B}\inf_{a\in A}d_\mathcal Z(a,b) \right\}.dHZ​(A,B)=max{a∈Asup​b∈Binf​dZ​(a,b),b∈Bsup​a∈Ainf​dZ​(a,b)}.
(34)

The Gromov--Hausdorff distance removes the common ambient space by minimizing this quantity over all isometric embeddings into a third space:

dGH(X,Y)=inf⁡Z,ϕ,ψdHZ(ϕ(X),ψ(Y)).d_{\mathrm{GH}}(\X,\Y) = \inf_{\mathcal Z,\phi,\psi} d_{\mathrm H}^{\mathcal Z}(\phi(\X),\psi(\Y)).dGH​(X,Y)=Z,ϕ,ψinf​dHZ​(ϕ(X),ψ(Y)).
(35)

Equivalently, it is half the minimal distortion of a correspondence between X\XX and Y\YY Gromov, 2001Mémoli, 2007. This is a worst-case set distance: every point must be matched with small distortion. Gromov--Wasserstein replaces correspondences by probability couplings and worst-case distortion by averaged distortion. It is therefore better adapted to noisy sampled shapes and weighted graphs, but it can ignore small sets of mass that would dominate the Hausdorff distance.

Quantum Optimal Transport¶

Quantum optimal transport replaces probability vectors by density matrices and scalar couplings by positive operators on a tensor product space. This is the right language when the transported objects are matrix-valued signals, covariance-like descriptors or quantum states, and it exposes a precise bridge between OT, non-commutative entropy and operator scaling Ning & Georgiou, 2014Chen et al., 2016Chen et al., 2020Peyré et al., 2019Caglioti et al., 2019Chakrabarti et al., 2019.

Finite-Dimensional States and Couplings¶

Definition: Hermitian and Density Matrices

Let Hn\mathbb H_nHn​ be the real vector space of n×nn\times nn×n Hermitian matrices,

Hn+={A∈Hn:A⪰0},Hn+,1={A∈Hn+:tr⁡(A)=1}.\mathbb H_n^+ = \{A\in\mathbb H_n:A\succeq0\}, \qquad \mathbb H_n^{+,1} = \{A\in\mathbb H_n^+:\operatorname{tr}(A)=1\}.Hn+​={A∈Hn​:A⪰0},Hn+,1​={A∈Hn+​:tr(A)=1}.
(36)

Elements of Hn+,1\mathbb H_n^{+,1}Hn+,1​ are density matrices.

A joint quantum state between Cn\mathbb C^nCn and Cm\mathbb C^mCm is a matrix T∈Hnm+T\in\mathbb H_{nm}^+T∈Hnm+​ acting on Cn⊗Cm\mathbb C^n\otimes\mathbb C^mCn⊗Cm. Its marginals are the partial traces, defined by duality through

tr⁡(F Tr⁡BT)=tr⁡((F⊗Im)T),tr⁡(G Tr⁡AT)=tr⁡((In⊗G)T).\operatorname{tr}(F\,\operatorname{Tr}_B T) = \operatorname{tr}((F\otimes I_m)T), \qquad \operatorname{tr}(G\,\operatorname{Tr}_A T) = \operatorname{tr}((I_n\otimes G)T).tr(FTrB​T)=tr((F⊗Im​)T),tr(GTrA​T)=tr((In​⊗G)T).
(37)

for all F∈HnF\in\mathbb H_nF∈Hn​ and G∈HmG\in\mathbb H_mG∈Hm​. Thus Tr⁡B(T)∈Hn+\operatorname{Tr}_B(T)\in\mathbb H_n^+TrB​(T)∈Hn+​ and Tr⁡A(T)∈Hm+\operatorname{Tr}_A(T)\in\mathbb H_m^+TrA​(T)∈Hm+​ play exactly the role of the two marginals of a classical coupling.

Definition: Finite-Dimensional Quantum OT

Let A∈Hn+,1A\in\mathbb H_n^{+,1}A∈Hn+,1​, B∈Hm+,1B\in\mathbb H_m^{+,1}B∈Hm+,1​ and let C∈HnmC\in\mathbb H_{nm}C∈Hnm​ be a Hermitian cost observable. The quantum OT value is the semidefinite program

QOT⁡C(A,B):=min⁡T∈Hnm+{tr⁡(CT):Tr⁡B(T)=A, Tr⁡A(T)=B}.\operatorname{QOT}_C(A,B) \eqdef \min_{T\in\mathbb H_{nm}^+} \left\{ \operatorname{tr}(CT): \operatorname{Tr}_B(T)=A,\ \operatorname{Tr}_A(T)=B \right\}.QOTC​(A,B):=T∈Hnm+​min​{tr(CT):TrB​(T)=A, TrA​(T)=B}.
(38)

The feasible set is never empty, since A⊗BA\otimes BA⊗B has marginals AAA and BBB.

Remark: Classical Diagonal Case

If AAA, BBB, CCC and TTT are all diagonal in fixed bases, then AAA and BBB are probability vectors, TTT is a nonnegative matrix and the partial-trace constraints reduce to the usual row and column sum constraints. Hence classical Kantorovich OT is the diagonal, commutative subcase of (38). The genuinely quantum feature is that TTT may contain off-diagonal coherences and entanglement.

Proposition: Quantum Kantorovich Duality

For A∈Hn+,1A\in\mathbb H_n^{+,1}A∈Hn+,1​ and B∈Hm+,1B\in\mathbb H_m^{+,1}B∈Hm+,1​, the dual of (38) is

QOT⁡C(A,B)=max⁡F∈Hn, G∈Hm{tr⁡(FA)+tr⁡(GB):F⊗Im+In⊗G⪯C}.\operatorname{QOT}_C(A,B) = \max_{F\in\mathbb H_n,\ G\in\mathbb H_m} \left\{ \operatorname{tr}(FA)+\operatorname{tr}(GB) : F\otimes I_m+I_n\otimes G\preceq C \right\}.QOTC​(A,B)=F∈Hn​, G∈Hm​max​{tr(FA)+tr(GB):F⊗Im​+In​⊗G⪯C}.
(39)

If A,BA,BA,B are positive definite, strong duality follows from Slater’s theorem; the semidefinite case follows by restricting to supports or by approximation.

Proof

Introduce Hermitian Lagrange multipliers FFF and GGG for the two marginal constraints. Using (37), the Lagrangian is

tr⁡(FA)+tr⁡(GB)+tr⁡ ⁣((C−F⊗Im−In⊗G)T).\operatorname{tr}(FA)+\operatorname{tr}(GB) + \operatorname{tr}\!\left((C-F\otimes I_m-I_n\otimes G)T\right).tr(FA)+tr(GB)+tr((C−F⊗Im​−In​⊗G)T).
(40)

Minimizing over T⪰0T\succeq0T⪰0 gives a finite lower bound if and only if C−F⊗Im−In⊗G⪰0C-F\otimes I_m-I_n\otimes G\succeq0C−F⊗Im​−In​⊗G⪰0, in which case the infimum in TTT is 0. When A,B≻0A,B\succ0A,B≻0, the coupling A⊗BA\otimes BA⊗B is strictly feasible, so Slater’s theorem gives equality of primal and dual values. The semidefinite case follows by restricting to supports or by approximation.

The dual potentials have the usual scalar gauge freedom: replacing (F,G)(F,G)(F,G) by (F+tIn,G−tIm)(F+tI_n,G-tI_m)(F+tIn​,G−tIm​) leaves both the constraint and the value unchanged because tr⁡(A)=tr⁡(B)=1\operatorname{tr}(A)=\operatorname{tr}(B)=1tr(A)=tr(B)=1.

Entropic Regularization and Bregman Iterations¶

Definition: von Neumann Quantum Entropy

For a density matrix or positive semidefinite matrix TTT, the shifted von Neumann entropy functional used here is

H(T)=tr⁡ ⁣(T(log⁡T−I)),∇H(T)=log⁡T,H(T) = \operatorname{tr}\!\left(T(\log T-I)\right), \qquad \nabla H(T)=\log T,H(T)=tr(T(logT−I)),∇H(T)=logT,
(41)

with the convention 0log⁡0=00\log0=00log0=0 on eigenvalues. This is the convex negative quantum entropy; on trace-one states it differs from the physical entropy by a sign and an additive constant.

For ϵ>0\epsilon>0ϵ>0 define

QOT⁡Cϵ(A,B)=min⁡T⪰0{tr⁡(CT)+ϵH(T):Tr⁡B(T)=A, Tr⁡A(T)=B}.\operatorname{QOT}_C^\epsilon(A,B) = \min_{T\succeq0} \left\{ \operatorname{tr}(CT)+\epsilon H(T): \operatorname{Tr}_B(T)=A,\ \operatorname{Tr}_A(T)=B \right\}.QOTCϵ​(A,B)=T⪰0min​{tr(CT)+ϵH(T):TrB​(T)=A, TrA​(T)=B}.
(42)

This is the non-commutative analogue of entropic OT: the Shannon entropy of a coupling is replaced by the trace entropy of a density matrix Peyré et al., 2019Chakrabarti et al., 2019.

Proposition: Entropic Quantum OT Duality

Assume A≻0A\succ0A≻0, B≻0B\succ0B≻0 and ϵ>0\epsilon>0ϵ>0. Then (42) has a unique positive minimizer. Its dual is

QOT⁡Cϵ(A,B)=max⁡F∈Hn, G∈Hm{tr⁡(FA)+tr⁡(GB)−ϵ tr⁡exp⁡ ⁣(F⊗Im+In⊗G−Cϵ)}.\operatorname{QOT}_C^\epsilon(A,B) = \max_{F\in\mathbb H_n,\ G\in\mathbb H_m} \left\{ \operatorname{tr}(FA)+\operatorname{tr}(GB) - \epsilon\, \operatorname{tr} \exp\!\left( \frac{F\otimes I_m+I_n\otimes G-C}{\epsilon} \right) \right\}.QOTCϵ​(A,B)=F∈Hn​, G∈Hm​max​{tr(FA)+tr(GB)−ϵtrexp(ϵF⊗Im​+In​⊗G−C​)}.
(43)

At optimality, primal and dual variables are linked by the Gibbs formula

Te(F,G)=exp⁡ ⁣(F⊗Im+In⊗G−Cϵ).T_e(F,G) = \exp\!\left( \frac{F\otimes I_m+I_n\otimes G-C}{\epsilon} \right).Te​(F,G)=exp(ϵF⊗Im​+In​⊗G−C​).
(44)

with Tr⁡B(Te)=A\operatorname{Tr}_B(T_e)=ATrB​(Te​)=A and Tr⁡A(Te)=B\operatorname{Tr}_A(T_e)=BTrA​(Te​)=B.

Proof

The feasible set is compact and nonempty, and it contains the positive definite point A⊗BA\otimes BA⊗B. The trace entropy is strictly convex on positive semidefinite matrices, hence the regularized primal has a unique minimizer. Slater’s condition justifies the Lagrange dual computation. The Fenchel identity

sup⁡T⪰0tr⁡(YT)−ϵH(T)=ϵ tr⁡exp⁡(Y/ϵ)\sup_{T\succeq0} \operatorname{tr}(YT)-\epsilon H(T) = \epsilon\,\operatorname{tr}\exp(Y/\epsilon)T⪰0sup​tr(YT)−ϵH(T)=ϵtrexp(Y/ϵ)
(45)

is the matrix analogue of the scalar exponential conjugacy. Applying it to the Lagrangian with Y=F⊗Im+In⊗G−CY=F\otimes I_m+I_n\otimes G-CY=F⊗Im​+In​⊗G−C gives (43), and the stationarity condition gives (44); differentiating the dual objective with respect to FFF and GGG yields the two marginal equations.

Writing K=exp⁡(−C/ϵ)K=\exp(-C/\epsilon)K=exp(−C/ϵ), the objective differs by a constant from ϵ\epsilonϵ times the quantum KL divergence

DH(T∣K)=tr⁡ ⁣(T(log⁡T−log⁡K)−T+K).D_H(T\mid K) = \operatorname{tr}\!\left( T(\log T-\log K)-T+K \right).DH​(T∣K)=tr(T(logT−logK)−T+K).
(46)

The exact quantum analogue of Sinkhorn is an implicit alternating Bregman projection scheme onto the affine marginal sets

MA={T⪰0:Tr⁡B(T)=A},MB={T⪰0:Tr⁡A(T)=B}.\mathcal M_A=\{T\succeq0:\operatorname{Tr}_B(T)=A\}, \qquad \mathcal M_B=\{T\succeq0:\operatorname{Tr}_A(T)=B\}.MA​={T⪰0:TrB​(T)=A},MB​={T⪰0:TrA​(T)=B}.
(47)
Proposition: Exact Bregman Projections

Assume A,B≻0A,B\succ0A,B≻0 and let K=exp⁡(−C/ϵ)K=\exp(-C/\epsilon)K=exp(−C/ϵ). The minimizer of (42) is equivalently the minimizer of DH(T∣K)D_H(T\mid K)DH​(T∣K) over MA∩MB\mathcal M_A\cap\mathcal M_BMA​∩MB​. Moreover, if a current positive definite matrix has Gibbs form Te(F,G)T_e(F,G)Te​(F,G), then its Bregman projection onto MA\mathcal M_AMA​ has the form Te(F+,G)T_e(F^+,G)Te​(F+,G), where F+F^+F+ is chosen so that Tr⁡BTe(F+,G)=A\operatorname{Tr}_B T_e(F^+,G)=ATrB​Te​(F+,G)=A. The projection onto MB\mathcal M_BMB​ is analogous. Thus, when each one-block marginal equation is solved exactly, alternating Bregman projections are equivalent to alternating block maximization of the dual (43).

Proof

Since log⁡K=−C/ϵ\log K=-C/\epsilonlogK=−C/ϵ,

tr⁡(CT)+ϵH(T)=ϵDH(T∣K)−ϵtr⁡(K).\operatorname{tr}(CT)+\epsilon H(T) = \epsilon D_H(T\mid K)-\epsilon\operatorname{tr}(K).tr(CT)+ϵH(T)=ϵDH​(T∣K)−ϵtr(K).
(48)

For the projection of a positive definite matrix SSS onto MA\mathcal M_AMA​, the affine set contains the positive definite point A⊗Im/mA\otimes I_m/mA⊗Im​/m. The entropy derivative is singular at the boundary, so the projection lies in the interior of the positive cone and the first variation has the form

log⁡T−log⁡S−Λ⊗Im=0,\log T-\log S-\Lambda\otimes I_m=0,logT−logS−Λ⊗Im​=0,
(49)

for a Hermitian multiplier Λ\LambdaΛ. Hence T=exp⁡(log⁡S+Λ⊗Im)T=\exp(\log S+\Lambda\otimes I_m)T=exp(logS+Λ⊗Im​). If S=Te(F,G)S=T_e(F,G)S=Te​(F,G), this is again Te(F+ϵΛ,G)T_e(F+\epsilon\Lambda,G)Te​(F+ϵΛ,G); the multiplier is fixed by the marginal equation. The same argument applies to MB\mathcal M_BMB​. Finally, the first-order optimality condition for maximizing (43) over one block is exactly the corresponding marginal equation, so the Bregman and block-dual views coincide.

In the diagonal case this proposition gives the usual multiplicative Sinkhorn updates. In the non-commutative case, however, the exact block equations

Tr⁡BTe(F,G)=A,Tr⁡ATe(F,G)=B\operatorname{Tr}_B T_e(F,G)=A, \qquad \operatorname{Tr}_A T_e(F,G)=BTrB​Te​(F,G)=A,TrA​Te​(F,G)=B
(50)

do not admit scalar division formulas, because the exponential of F⊗Im+In⊗G−CF\otimes I_m+I_n\otimes G-CF⊗Im​+In​⊗G−C cannot be separated unless the local potential commutes with the cost.

Gurvits Scaling and Quantum Sinkhorn¶

The algorithm often called quantum Sinkhorn comes from the operator-scaling literature of Gurvits and subsequent developments Gurvits, 2003Gurvits, 2004Georgiou & Pavon, 2015Garg & Oliveira, 2018. It replaces the true Gibbs coupling (44) by the symmetric factorization

Ts(F,G)=exp⁡ ⁣(Z2ϵ)exp⁡(−C/ϵ)exp⁡ ⁣(Z2ϵ)=(U⊗V)K(U⊗V),Z=F⊗Im+In⊗G,T_s(F,G) = \exp\!\left(\frac{Z}{2\epsilon}\right) \exp(-C/\epsilon) \exp\!\left(\frac{Z}{2\epsilon}\right) = (U\otimes V)K(U\otimes V), \qquad Z=F\otimes I_m+I_n\otimes G,Ts​(F,G)=exp(2ϵZ​)exp(−C/ϵ)exp(2ϵZ​)=(U⊗V)K(U⊗V),Z=F⊗Im​+In​⊗G,
(51)

where U=exp⁡(F/(2ϵ))U=\exp(F/(2\epsilon))U=exp(F/(2ϵ)), V=exp⁡(G/(2ϵ))V=\exp(G/(2\epsilon))V=exp(G/(2ϵ)) and K=exp⁡(−C/ϵ)K=\exp(-C/\epsilon)K=exp(−C/ϵ). If [Z,C]=0[Z,C]=0[Z,C]=0, then Ts(F,G)=Te(F,G)T_s(F,G)=T_e(F,G)Ts​(F,G)=Te​(F,G); otherwise this is a Strang-type symmetric surrogate.

Fix a Choi convention and let K:Hm→Hn\mathcal K:\mathbb H_m\to\mathbb H_nK:Hm​→Hn​ be the completely positive map represented by the positive Choi matrix KKK; let K⋆\mathcal K^\starK⋆ be its Hilbert--Schmidt adjoint. Up to the transpose dictated by the chosen Choi convention, the marginal equations for the symmetric coupling take the operator-scaling form

U K(V2) U=A,V K⋆(U2) V=B,U\,\mathcal K(V^2)\,U=A, \qquad V\,\mathcal K^\star(U^2)\,V=B,UK(V2)U=A,VK⋆(U2)V=B,
(52)

and can be enforced by the congruence normalizations

RV=K(V2),U←RV−1/2(RV1/2ARV1/2)1/2RV−1/2,SU=K⋆(U2),V←SU−1/2(SU1/2BSU1/2)1/2SU−1/2.\begin{aligned} R_V&=\mathcal K(V^2), & U&\leftarrow R_V^{-1/2} \left(R_V^{1/2} A R_V^{1/2}\right)^{1/2} R_V^{-1/2}, \\ S_U&=\mathcal K^\star(U^2), & V&\leftarrow S_U^{-1/2} \left(S_U^{1/2} B S_U^{1/2}\right)^{1/2} S_U^{-1/2}. \end{aligned}RV​SU​​=K(V2),=K⋆(U2),​UV​←RV−1/2​(RV1/2​ARV1/2​)1/2RV−1/2​,←SU−1/2​(SU1/2​BSU1/2​)1/2SU−1/2​.​
(53)

These inverse square roots are well-defined when K≻0K\succ0K≻0 and U,V,A,B≻0U,V,A,B\succ0U,V,A,B≻0. This is Gurvits/operator scaling with prescribed targets; when all matrices are diagonal it reduces to classical Sinkhorn scaling, and when the targets are proportional to identities it matches the usual bistochastic operator-scaling normalization, up to the conventional trace normalization.

Remark: Gurvits Scaling Is Not the Exact Bregman Scheme

The exact Bregman step would enforce the marginals of Te(F,G)=exp⁡((Z−C)/ϵ)T_e(F,G)=\exp((Z-C)/\epsilon)Te​(F,G)=exp((Z−C)/ϵ) and would be a block maximization of the true concave dual (43). Gurvits scaling instead enforces the marginals of the surrogate

Ts=exp⁡ ⁣(Z2ϵ)exp⁡(−C/ϵ)exp⁡ ⁣(Z2ϵ).T_s= \exp\!\left(\frac{Z}{2\epsilon}\right) \exp(-C/\epsilon) \exp\!\left(\frac{Z}{2\epsilon}\right).Ts​=exp(2ϵZ​)exp(−C/ϵ)exp(2ϵZ​).
(54)

The two coincide in the commuting/diagonal regime, but in general the Baker--Campbell--Hausdorff commutator terms do not vanish. Gurvits scaling should therefore be understood as a tractable symmetric operator-scaling approximation to entropic quantum OT, not as the literal alternating KL projection algorithm.

Remark: Operator-Valued Couplings

The same definitions extend formally from matrices to separable Hilbert spaces by replacing density matrices with positive trace-class operators of trace one, observables with bounded self-adjoint operators and partial traces defined by duality against local bounded observables. If Π(A,B)\Pi(A,B)Π(A,B) denotes positive trace-class operators with partial traces AAA and BBB, a bounded cost observable CCC gives the problem inf⁡T∈Π(A,B)Tr⁡(CT)\inf_{T\in\Pi(A,B)}\operatorname{Tr}(CT)infT∈Π(A,B)​Tr(CT). For unbounded positive costs one must define the energy through the quadratic form or spectral truncations, and in the entropic case one must ensure that exp⁡(−C/ϵ)\exp(-C/\epsilon)exp(−C/ϵ) is trace class and that the partial traces of the candidate coupling are well-defined. The matrix formulas above are therefore the clean finite-dimensional core; the operator version adds domain and compactness assumptions rather than a different algebraic structure.

References¶
  1. Maas, J., Rumpf, M., Schönlieb, C., & Simon, S. (2015). A generalized model for optimal transport of images including dissipation and density modulation. ESAIM: Mathematical Modelling and Numerical Analysis, 49(6), 1745–1769.
  2. Maas, J., Rumpf, M., & Simon, S. (2016). Generalized optimal transport with singular sources. arXiv Preprint arXiv:1607.01186.
  3. Dolbeault, J., Nazaret, B., & Savaré, G. (2009). A new class of transport distances between measures. Calculus of Variations and Partial Differential Equations, 34(2), 193–231.
  4. Mielke, A. (2013). Geodesic convexity of the relative entropy in reversible Markov chains. Calculus of Variations and Partial Differential Equations, 48(1–2), 1–31.
  5. Ning, L., & Georgiou, T. T. (2014). Metrics for matrix-valued measures via test functions. 53rd IEEE Conference on Decision and Control, 2642–2647.
  6. Jiang, X., Ning, L., & Georgiou, T. T. (2012). Distances and Riemannian metrics for multivariate spectral densities. IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control, 57(7), 1723–1735.
  7. Ning, L., Georgiou, T. T., & Tannenbaum, A. (2015). On matrix-valued Monge–Kantorovich optimal mass transport. IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control, 60(2), 373–382. 10.1109/TAC.2014.2350171
  8. Chen, Y., Georgiou, T. T., & Tannenbaum, A. (2016). Matrix optimal mass transport: a quantum mechanical approach. arXiv Preprint arXiv:1610.03041.
  9. Chen, Y., Gangbo, W., Georgiou, T. T., & Tannenbaum, A. (2020). On the matrix Monge-Kantorovich problem. European Journal of Applied Mathematics, 31(4), 574–600. 10.1017/S0956792519000172
  10. Carlen, E. A., & Maas, J. (2014). An analog of the 2-Wasserstein metric in non-commutative probability under which the fermionic Fokker–Planck equation is gradient flow for the entropy. Communications in Mathematical Physics, 331(3), 887–926.
  11. Peyré, G., Chizat, L., Vialard, F.-X., & Solomon, J. (2019). Quantum entropic regularization of matrix-valued optimal transport. European Journal of Applied Mathematics, 30(6), 1079–1102. 10.1017/S0956792517000274
  12. Loiola, E. M., de Abreu, N. M. M., Boaventura-Netto, P. O., Hahn, P., & Querido, T. (2007). A survey for the quadratic assignment problem. European Journal of Operational Research, 176(2), 657–690. 10.1016/j.ejor.2005.09.032
  13. Lyzinski, V., Fishkind, D. E., Fiori, M., Vogelstein, J. T., Priebe, C. E., & Sapiro, G. (2016). Graph matching: relax at your own risk. IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence, 38(1), 60–73.
  14. Mémoli, F. (2011). Gromov–Wasserstein distances and the metric approach to object matching. Foundations of Computational Mathematics, 11(4), 417–487.
  15. Sturm, K.-T. (2012). The space of spaces: curvature bounds and gradient flows on the space of metric measure spaces (Preprint 1208.0434). arXiv.
